# IL-42 suppresses activated CD8 T cells by lowering granzyme B without cell-contact dependence

## Citation

Lee H, Raman S, Ortega M. Journal of Experimental Immunology (2026).  
DOI: 10.1234/jei.2026.0042

## Background

IL-42 is a candidate cytokine suspected to tune effector T-cell responses. The authors asked whether IL-42 directly weakens activated CD8 T-cell function and, if so, whether the effect depends on cell-cell contact.

## Experimental setup

- Primary human CD8 T cells were activated with anti-CD3/CD28 beads.
- Jurkat cells were used as a parallel cell-line model.
- Recombinant IL-42 was added at 0, 5, or 20 ng/mL.
- A flow-cytometry activation assay measured CD69, IFN-gamma, and granzyme B after 24 hours.
- Transwell plates separated secreting cells from responder cells to test contact dependence.
- An anti-IL-42 neutralizing antibody was used as a rescue control.

## Results

1. At 20 ng/mL, IL-42 reduced granzyme B-positive primary CD8 cells by 38 percent and IFN-gamma-positive cells by 24 percent.
2. CD69 changed by less than 5 percent, suggesting activation entry was largely preserved while effector output dropped.
3. Similar suppression was observed in the transwell condition, which argues for a contact-independent mechanism.
4. Jurkat cells showed a weaker granzyme B reduction of about 15 percent, so the effect was stronger in primary cells.
5. Anti-IL-42 antibody restored granzyme B to near baseline.

## Interpretation

The paper argues that IL-42 suppresses activated CD8 effector function primarily by lowering granzyme B rather than by preventing early activation. The transwell result is used to support a soluble, contact-independent mode of suppression.

## Limitations

- Only one 24-hour time point was tested.
- No in vivo validation was provided.
- The receptor for IL-42 was not identified.
- Jurkat cells may not reproduce the full magnitude of the primary-cell effect.
